summary: I was born in Ujjain, Madhya Pradesh, India in 1998. Although my family moved to California before I was born, I came into this world in Ujjain because my parents wanted me to be born in India. It holds a special place in my story as the starting point of my life before I moved back and forth between India and the US.

Madhya Pradesh is the Indian state where I was born, specifically in the city of Ujjain. After spending my early years in California, my family returned to Madhya Pradesh when I was in fifth grade, and I continued my education there. It was in Madhya Pradesh that I settled into middle and high school, embracing my Indian identity more deeply.

India is where I was born and where I spent important parts of my childhood and adolescence. My family moved back to India after living in California, and I adapted to life there, attending school and embracing Indian culture. Even though I have an American passport, India shaped much of who I am, from language to traditions like Diwali and Bollywood music.

California was my home during my early childhood until fifth grade, as my father moved there in 1994 to work for Microsoft. I attended elementary school in Fremont, California, and my early experiences included watching PBS Kids shows like Arthur and Sesame Street. California represents the American part of my upbringing before we moved back to India.

My father moved to California in 1994 to work for Microsoft, which was the reason our family initially settled in the US. His job at Microsoft shaped much of our early life in California before we returned to India. This connection to Microsoft was the foundation for my American upbringing during my early years.

I went to elementary school in Fremont, California, where I spent my weekdays before moving back to India. Fremont was where I balanced my American school life with my Indian cultural upbringing, including reciting Sanskrit shlokas in the evenings. It was a unique place where my dual identities began to form.

Since I was a child, I recited ancient Sanskrit shlokas in the evenings while attending school in California. Sanskrit was part of my cultural education, connecting me to my Indian heritage even while living in the US. It was one of the three languages I grew up speaking, alongside English and Hindi.

During my elementary school years in Fremont, California, I watched Arthur on PBS Kids after school. It was one of the shows that connected me to American culture while I was growing up. Watching Arthur alongside Sesame Street helped me balance my Indian and American identities.

Sesame Street was a part of my daily routine after school when I lived in Fremont, California. Watching it on PBS Kids helped me learn about American culture and language during my early years. It was a comforting and familiar show as I navigated my bicultural childhood.

PBS Kids was the channel where I watched shows like Arthur and Sesame Street during my elementary school days in Fremont, California. It played a role in my early exposure to American culture and language. These programs were part of my daily routine and helped me feel connected to the US while living in a multicultural household.

Diwali was the big holiday in my family, reflecting our Indian traditions even while living in the US. Celebrating Diwali connected me to my cultural roots amidst a predominantly American environment. It was a time when our house was filled with the warmth of Indian customs and family gatherings.

Our house was always filled with old Bollywood music, which was a big part of my cultural upbringing. Even though I lived in California for part of my childhood, Bollywood connected me to India and its traditions. It was one of the ways my family kept our heritage alive while living abroad.

Indian classical music was another important part of my family's cultural environment growing up. Alongside Bollywood music, it filled our house and helped me stay connected to my Indian roots. These musical traditions were a constant presence during my childhood in both India and the US.

Although Diwali was our main holiday, my mom brought home a little tinsel tree for Christmas, and we went trick-or-treating every year. Celebrating Christmas in California was part of my experience growing up in the US, blending Indian and American traditions. It showed how my family embraced multiple cultures during my childhood.

I knew the Indian national anthem from a young age, which was part of my connection to India despite living in the US. Learning it alongside the American Pledge of Allegiance reflected my bicultural identity. It symbolized the duality of my upbringing between India and America.

I learned the American Pledge of Allegiance in two languages, which was part of my early education in California. It stood alongside my knowledge of the Indian national anthem, highlighting my dual identity. Reciting the pledge connected me to American culture during my formative years.

I came to the University of Minnesota to study after finishing high school in India. It was at the U of M campus that I began to deeply question my identity as Indian American. Being there made me reflect on what it means to belong to two cultures and how I fit into both worlds.
